protocols.description: FIDO2/WebAuthn registration (the 'create' ceremony) establishes a new public key credential bound to a specific relying party (RP). The RP server generates a challenge (a cryptographically random byte sequence of at least 16 bytes) and sends it to the client along with RP information (rp.id, rp.name), user information (user.id, user.name, user.displayName), and credential creation options (pubKeyCredParams specifying acceptable algorithms such as ES256 or RS256, authenticatorSelection criteria, attestation preference). The client calls navigator.credentials.create() which triggers the WebAuthn API. The authenticator generates a new asymmetric key pair, stores the private key internally (in a TPM, secure enclave, or platform authenticator), and returns a PublicKeyCredential containing the attestation object (with the new public key, credential ID, and attestation statement) and the client data (challenge, origin, type). The RP server validates the challenge, origin, attestation statement (if requested), and stores the credential ID and public key associated with the user account. This process effectively replaces password-based account creation with a phishing-resistant cryptographic credential.
protocols.security_considerations: The challenge must be cryptographically random (at least 16 bytes per W3C WebAuthn spec) and single-use to prevent replay attacks. Validate that the origin in the clientDataJSON matches the expected RP origin exactly (including scheme and port) to prevent phishing. The rp.id must match the effective domain of the RP to prevent cross-origin credential creation. Choose the attestation conveyance preference carefully: 'none' (recommended for most consumer apps) does not provide authenticator provenance, 'indirect' allows the authenticator to generate privacy-preserving attestation, and 'direct' provides full attestation (required for high-assurance environments but may impact user privacy). If verifying attestation, validate the attestation statement against FIDO Metadata Service (MDS) to check authenticator certification status and known vulnerabilities. Store the credential public key and credentialId securely; these are equivalent to a user's authentication factor. Implement a maximum number of credentials per user to prevent storage exhaustion. For platform authenticators, educate users about device loss recovery procedures. Use the excludeCredentials option to prevent duplicate registration of the same authenticator.
protocols.rfc: W3C Web Authentication Level 2, FIDO2 CTAP2
protocols.sequence_diagram:
User                    Browser/Client           RP Server            Authenticator
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |---Register Request------>|                          |                     |
  |                          |---Begin Registration---->|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Challenge + Options----|                     |
  |                          |  (challenge, rp,         |                     |
  |                          |   user, pubKeyCredParams,|                     |
  |                          |   attestation)           |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---credentials.create()---|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |<--User Verification------|---(gesture/biometric/PIN)------------->|
  |---Approve--------------->|                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |  [Generate key pair |
  |                          |                          |   store private key]|
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Attestation Object + ClientData------|
  |                          |  (credentialId,          |                     |
  |                          |   publicKey,             |                     |
  |                          |   attestation)           |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---Send Attestation------>|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |  [Validate challenge,    |                     |
  |                          |   origin, attestation,   |                     |
  |                          |   store credential]      |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Registration Success---|                     |

protocols.description: FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication (the 'get assertion' ceremony) verifies a user's identity using a previously registered public key credential. The RP server generates a new challenge and sends it to the client along with the allowCredentials list (credential IDs the user registered previously) and optional user verification requirements (required, preferred, or discouraged). The client calls navigator.credentials.get() which triggers the authenticator to locate a matching credential. The authenticator prompts the user for verification (biometric, PIN, or presence test depending on the authenticatorSelection policy), retrieves the private key associated with the credential, and signs the authenticatorData (containing the rpIdHash, flags including user presence and user verification indicators, and a signature counter) concatenated with the clientDataHash. The RP server receives the assertion, validates the challenge, verifies the origin and rpIdHash, checks the user presence/verification flags, validates the signature using the stored public key, and checks the signature counter to detect cloned authenticators. This entire process is phishing-resistant because the authenticator cryptographically binds the assertion to the RP's origin.
protocols.security_considerations: The challenge must be fresh, cryptographically random, and single-use. Verify the rpIdHash in the authenticatorData matches the SHA-256 hash of the RP ID to confirm the authenticator is responding to the correct RP. Check the UP (User Present) flag is set, and if userVerification was required, verify the UV flag is also set. Monitor the signature counter: if it is lower than the previously stored value, the authenticator may have been cloned (this is especially relevant for hardware security keys; platform authenticators may not increment counters reliably). For passwordless authentication (discoverable credentials/resident keys), the allowCredentials list is empty and the authenticator identifies the user from its internal storage, so ensure userVerification is set to 'required' to prevent unauthorized access from someone with physical access to the device. Implement account lockout or rate limiting after repeated failed authentication attempts. The clientDataJSON.origin validation prevents phishing because a phishing site on a different domain will produce a different origin, and the authenticator will not find matching credentials. Consider implementing token binding or related channel binding mechanisms where supported.
protocols.rfc: W3C Web Authentication Level 2, FIDO2 CTAP2
protocols.sequence_diagram:
User                    Browser/Client           RP Server            Authenticator
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |---Login Request--------->|                          |                     |
  |                          |---Begin Authentication-->|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Challenge + Options----|                     |
  |                          |  (challenge,             |                     |
  |                          |   allowCredentials,      |                     |
  |                          |   userVerification)      |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---credentials.get()------|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |<--User Verification------|---(gesture/biometric/PIN)------------->|
  |---Approve--------------->|                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |  [Find credential,  |
  |                          |                          |   sign challenge    |
  |                          |                          |   with private key] |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Assertion (signature, authenticatorData)---|
  |                          |  (credentialId,          |                     |
  |                          |   authenticatorData,     |                     |
  |                          |   signature,             |                     |
  |                          |   clientDataJSON)        |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---Send Assertion-------->|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |  [Validate challenge,    |                     |
  |                          |   origin, rpIdHash,      |                     |
  |                          |   flags, counter,        |                     |
  |                          |   verify signature]      |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Authentication OK------|                     |

protocols.description: Passkeys are synced discoverable credentials that extend FIDO2/WebAuthn to provide a seamless, passwordless, and phishing-resistant authentication experience that works across devices. Unlike traditional FIDO2 credentials that are bound to a single authenticator (hardware key or platform), passkeys are stored in a platform credential manager (Apple iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, or Microsoft Windows Hello) and synchronized across the user's devices via end-to-end encrypted cloud sync. Passkeys are discoverable credentials (formerly called 'resident keys'), meaning the authenticator can identify the user without an allowCredentials hint, enabling true passwordless sign-in with just a username or even no username at all (usernameless authentication). Cross-device authentication is supported via FIDO2 hybrid transport (caBLE/hybrid), where a user can authenticate on a desktop by scanning a QR code with their phone, using the phone's passkey via Bluetooth proximity verification. The registration and authentication ceremonies follow the standard WebAuthn flow but with the authenticator being the platform's synced credential manager rather than a hardware token.
protocols.security_considerations: Passkey sync security depends on the platform credential manager's end-to-end encryption implementation. Apple, Google, and Microsoft each have different key protection, recovery, and cross-device sync mechanisms. The sync introduces a new attack vector: account takeover of the platform account (Apple ID, Google Account, Microsoft Account) could compromise all synced passkeys. Require strong MFA on platform accounts that hold passkeys. Hybrid (cross-device) authentication via Bluetooth and QR codes introduces proximity verification, but verify that the implementation correctly validates Bluetooth proximity to prevent relay attacks. For high-assurance use cases, organizations may prefer non-synced (device-bound) passkeys by setting the authenticatorAttachment to 'platform' and using enterprise attestation. Account recovery is critical: if a user loses access to all devices and their platform account, they lose all passkeys. Implement alternative recovery flows (backup codes, identity verification, admin recovery). Passkeys do not support the concept of revocation in the traditional sense; the RP can delete the credential from its server, but the passkey remains in the user's credential manager until they manually delete it. Consider supplementing passkeys with risk-based authentication signals (device posture, location, behavior) for defense-in-depth.
protocols.rfc: W3C Web Authentication Level 2, FIDO Alliance Passkey Specifications
protocols.sequence_diagram:
User                    Browser/Client           RP Server            Platform Credential Mgr
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |---Sign In (no password)->|                          |                     |
  |                          |---Begin Authentication-->|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Challenge (no ----------|                     |
  |                          |   allowCredentials,      |                     |
  |                          |   userVerification=      |                     |
  |                          |   required)              |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---credentials.get()------|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |<--Select passkey---------|--------------------------|----------->|
  |  (account chooser or     |                          |                     |
  |   auto-fill UI)          |                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |---Biometric/PIN--------->|                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |  [Retrieve synced   |
  |                          |                          |   private key,      |
  |                          |                          |   sign challenge]   |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Assertion (signed)-----|---------------|
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---Send Assertion-------->|                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |  [Validate: challenge,   |                     |
  |                          |   origin, rpIdHash,      |                     |
  |                          |   UV flag, signature]    |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Authenticated----------|                     |

protocols.description: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) User Provisioning automates the creation, updating, and deactivation of user accounts across systems using a standardized REST API. The SCIM client (typically an Identity Provider or Identity Governance platform such as Azure AD, Okta, or SailPoint) synchronizes user lifecycle events to a SCIM service provider (the target application). User creation is performed via HTTP POST to the /Users endpoint with a JSON payload containing the user's attributes according to the SCIM User schema (userName, name, emails, groups, active status, etc.). Updates are performed via HTTP PUT (full replacement) or HTTP PATCH (partial update per RFC 7644 Section 3.5.2) to /Users/{id}. Deactivation is typically done by PATCHing the active attribute to false rather than deleting the resource. The SCIM protocol uses ETags for optimistic concurrency control, standard HTTP status codes, and supports filtering (/Users?filter=userName eq "john") for efficient synchronization. SCIM 2.0 defines a core schema (RFC 7643) and protocol (RFC 7644) with extension schemas for enterprise users (employeeNumber, department, manager, etc.).
protocols.security_considerations: SCIM endpoints handle sensitive identity data and must be protected with strong authentication. Use OAuth 2.0 Bearer tokens (RFC 6750) for SCIM API authentication; basic authentication is allowed by the spec but discouraged for production. Enforce TLS 1.2+ on all SCIM endpoints. Implement rate limiting to prevent enumeration attacks via the filter endpoint. Validate and sanitize all filter expressions to prevent injection attacks (some SCIM implementations are vulnerable to LDAP-injection-like attacks in filter parsing). Implement proper access control: SCIM clients should only be able to manage users within their authorized scope (tenant isolation in multi-tenant deployments). Log all SCIM operations for audit (user created, updated, deactivated, deleted). Handle eventual consistency carefully: network failures during provisioning can result in orphaned accounts or partially provisioned users. Implement idempotent operations using externalId for correlation between source and target systems. Protect against bulk enumeration: the /Users endpoint with no filter should require pagination and may be restricted. Monitor for anomalous provisioning patterns such as mass deactivation (which could indicate a compromised SCIM client).
protocols.sequence_diagram:
IdP / HR System         SCIM Service Provider    Target Application
(SCIM Client)           (SCIM Server)            (User Store)
  |                          |                          |
  |---POST /Users----------->|                          |
  |  (userName, name,        |                          |
  |   emails, active=true,   |                          |
  |   externalId)            |                          |
  |                          |---Create User----------->|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--201 Created (User)-----|                          |
  |  (id, meta.created)      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [Later: attribute       |                          |
  |   change detected]       |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---PATCH /Users/{id}----->|                          |
  |  (op: replace,           |                          |
  |   path: emails,          |                          |
  |   value: new@email.com)  |                          |
  |                          |---Update User----------->|
  |<--200 OK (User)----------|                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [User terminated in HR] |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---PATCH /Users/{id}----->|                          |
  |  (op: replace,           |                          |
  |   path: active,          |                          |
  |   value: false)          |                          |
  |                          |---Deactivate User------->|
  |<--200 OK (User)----------|                          |

protocols.description: SCIM Group Management enables automated synchronization of group memberships between identity providers and service providers. Groups in SCIM (RFC 7643 Section 4.2) are represented as resources with a displayName and a members array containing references to User resources (value=user ID, $ref=user URI, display=user display name). The SCIM client creates groups via HTTP POST to /Groups, updates membership via HTTP PATCH (adding or removing members using the 'add' and 'remove' operations on the members path), and can replace the entire group with HTTP PUT. Membership changes typically flow from the source of truth (IdP, HR system, or governance platform) to target applications. Nested groups (groups containing other groups) are supported but not universally implemented across SCIM service providers. Group sync is essential for maintaining consistent role-based access control (RBAC) across applications, ensuring that when a user is added to or removed from a security group in the IdP, their access in downstream applications is updated accordingly within the provisioning cycle.
protocols.security_considerations: Group membership changes directly affect authorization decisions across all connected applications. A compromised SCIM client that can modify group memberships can escalate privileges by adding users to admin or privileged groups. Implement strict authorization controls on the SCIM server: restrict which groups each SCIM client can manage and prevent modifications to critical security groups (e.g., Global Admins) via SCIM without additional approval workflows. Log all group membership changes with before/after state for forensic auditing. Implement safeguards against accidental mass removal: if a SCIM sync would remove more than a configurable threshold (e.g., 50%) of members from a group, require manual approval or block the operation. Validate the consistency of member references: $ref URIs and value IDs should resolve to valid users. Handle race conditions where a user is deprovisioned while simultaneously being added to a group. Be aware that SCIM PATCH semantics for multi-valued attributes (like members) vary across implementations; test interoperability carefully. Consider using ETags for optimistic concurrency control to prevent lost updates when multiple SCIM clients modify the same group.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
IdP / Governance        SCIM Service Provider    Target Application
(SCIM Client)           (SCIM Server)            (Group Store)
  |                          |                          |
  |---POST /Groups---------->|                          |
  |  (displayName,           |                          |
  |   members: [user1,       |                          |
  |   user2])                |                          |
  |                          |---Create Group---------->|
  |<--201 Created (Group)----|                          |
  |  (id, meta.created)      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [New member added       |                          |
  |   in source system]      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---PATCH /Groups/{id}---->|                          |
  |  (op: add,               |                          |
  |   path: members,         |                          |
  |   value: [{value:        |                          |
  |   user3_id}])            |                          |
  |                          |---Add Member------------>|
  |<--200 OK (Group)---------|                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [Member removed         |                          |
  |   in source system]      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---PATCH /Groups/{id}---->|                          |
  |  (op: remove,            |                          |
  |   path: members          |                          |
  |   [value eq user2_id])   |                          |
  |                          |---Remove Member--------->|
  |<--200 OK (Group)---------|                          |

protocols.description: SCIM Bulk Operations (RFC 7644 Section 3.7) enable batch processing of multiple SCIM requests in a single HTTP call to the /Bulk endpoint. The SCIM client sends an HTTP POST containing an array of Operations, each specifying a method (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), a path (/Users, /Groups, /Users/{id}), optional bulkId (for cross-referencing within the batch), and the operation data. The server processes the operations and returns a response containing the result of each operation with its corresponding status code. Bulk operations support cross-referencing via bulkId, allowing a user creation in one operation to be referenced by a group membership addition in a subsequent operation within the same batch. The server specifies a maxOperations limit and a maxPayloadSize limit in its ServiceProviderConfig. Bulk operations are essential for large-scale provisioning scenarios such as initial directory synchronization, organizational restructuring, mass onboarding/offboarding, and periodic full-sync reconciliation between source and target systems.
protocols.security_considerations: Bulk operations amplify the impact of a compromised SCIM client because a single request can create, modify, or delete hundreds or thousands of identities. Implement strict rate limiting on the /Bulk endpoint. Set conservative maxOperations (e.g., 100-1000) and maxPayloadSize limits in the ServiceProviderConfig. Enforce the same authorization checks on each individual operation within the bulk as would be applied to individual SCIM requests; do not shortcut authorization for bulk. Implement transactional semantics carefully: the SCIM spec allows servers to choose between fail-on-error (stop processing after the first error, specified via the failOnErrors attribute) or best-effort (process all operations and report individual results). For critical operations, use failOnErrors=1 to prevent partial state changes. Log each operation within the bulk individually for audit purposes, not just the bulk request as a whole. Monitor bulk operations for anomalous patterns such as mass deletion or mass privilege escalation. Implement dry-run or preview capabilities for bulk operations in high-assurance environments. Be aware of timeout implications: large bulk requests may exceed server-side request timeouts, requiring chunking or async processing with status polling.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
SCIM Client             SCIM Server              User/Group Store
  |                          |                          |
  |---POST /Bulk------------>|                          |
  |  {                       |                          |
  |    schemas: [Bulk],      |                          |
  |    failOnErrors: 1,      |                          |
  |    Operations: [         |                          |
  |      {method: POST,      |                          |
  |       path: /Users,      |                          |
  |       bulkId: user1,     |                          |
  |       data: {userName..}}|                          |
  |      {method: POST,      |                          |
  |       path: /Groups,     |                          |
  |       data: {members:    |                          |
  |         [bulkId:user1]}} |                          |
  |      {method: PATCH,     |                          |
  |       path: /Users/abc,  |                          |
  |       data: {active:     |                          |
  |         false}}          |                          |
  |    ]                     |                          |
  |  }                       |                          |
  |                          |---Process op 1: Create-->|
  |                          |---Process op 2: Create-->|
  |                          |  (resolve bulkId ref)    |
  |                          |---Process op 3: Update-->|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Bulk Response----------|                          |
  |  {Operations: [          |                          |
  |    {status: 201,         |                          |
  |     bulkId: user1,       |                          |
  |     location: /Users/x}  |                          |
  |    {status: 201}         |                          |
  |    {status: 200}         |                          |
  |  ]}                      |                          |
